text: Well, twist my nipples and send me to
Alaska because the Bone Temple was
absolutely not what I expected. Let's be
honest here. Now that all the hype and
buildup have died down, I think we can
all accept that 28 years later wasn't
exactly the triumphant return to form
that the trailer suggested. Yeah, it
definitely had some great moments that
were helped along by superb
performances, but it was kind of let
down by a disjointed narrative, plot
conveniences galore, and an ending that
had most people scratching their heads.
And let's also be honest that most of us
probably didn't expect much from the
sequel, The Bone Temple, directed as it
was by the same woman who gave us The
Marvels.
>> But here's the funny thing. It's pretty
[ __ ] good, actually.
>> That doesn't make sense.
>> Maybe not movie of the year kind of good
like some people are painting it as. And
yeah, it kind of feels like an expansion
pack for the previous film rather than a
complete narrative of its own, but it's
definitely a compelling horror movie
with some truly gruesome moments that
keeps you guessing about where it's all
leading. And it's elevated by one of the
best villain performances in recent
years. Now, I'm going to try and keep
this review mostly spoiler-free, but to
understand the plot, I have to give you
some idea of what happens in this film.
So, consider yourself warned. Anyway,
the story picks up right after the
events of the last film. Spike's been
captured by Jimmy and his gang of
jimmies and offered a chance to join
them, provided he can kill one of them
in hand-to-hand combat, which he does.
Lord Jimmy turns out to be a terrifying
figure who rules through a combination
of charismatic leadership and absolute
fear, wandering the countryside with his
fellow jimmies and just murdering anyone
who crosses their path. How exactly have
they managed to survive for years with
no equipment, supplies, shelter, or
fixed defenses? Why have they never been
overwhelmed by the infected while
traveling out in the open? Why has
nobody else ever tried to take them out
when they're clearly a threat? Don't
know. Anyway, while all this is going
on, Dr. Kelson is experimenting on
massive Dong Man, who's beginning to
show signs of humanity and understanding
after being repeatedly tranquilized by
him. Could there possibly be a way to
reverse the effects of the rage virus?
The two groups are eventually put on a
collision course when one of the Jimmies
spots Kelson and massive Dong Man
dancing together and believes that he's
literally the incarnation of Satan on
Earth, which works out nicely because
Jimmy and his gang are devil
worshippers. But what will happen when
Lord Jimmy confronts Kelson? Will
Massive Dong Man ever regain his
humanity? And will Spike find a way to
stand up to the terrifying leader of
their group? I said earlier that The
Bone Temple isn't the movie I expected.
And while that can be a good thing to do
the unexpected and keep your audience
guessing, it does create a few problems
that I'll get into shortly. I will say
that if you're expecting a continuation
of Spike's character arc, you're
probably going to be disappointed here
because this movie really isn't his
story. He's more of an observer this
time around because the real meat of the
narrative is focused on Kelson and Lord
Jimmy. And on that subject, damn son,
they did a hell of a job with that
character. What I always find disturbing
about a good antagonist is that feeling
like you never quite know what they're
going to do at any moment. And Jimmy's a
perfect example of this. At times
paternal, charismatic, and even funny,
at others, quiet and self-reflective,
and then at other times brutally
sadistic and cruel, he inspires a kind
of fanatical loyalty in his gang because
they're just too scared to challenge
him. It's never really explained why he
styles himself after Jimmy Savo, and
given context to American audiences,
would probably take too long anyway. So,
I guess we're just supposed to go with
it. And yeah, if I was going to be super
critical of the character, I'd say he
does allow himself to be baited into a
very obvious trap. But then, he is a bit
of a [ __ ] Froot Loop, so who knows?
Either way, he's absolutely nailed by
Jack O' Conno, who seems to be having
the year of his life playing charismatic
but terrifying antagonists that really
stick with you. There's not many young
actors who can hold their own against
veterans like Rafe Fines. But this guy
doesn't just hold his own, he dominates
every scene he's in. The Bone Temple is
basically his story, an exploration of
what true evil looks like and how it
justifies itself. And that being the
case, mission [ __ ] accomplished. It's
not entirely great on the acting front,
though. Casting Aaron Kellyman, who most
of you probably know as Freckle Jesus
from Falcon and the Winter Soldier, was
a risky move that doesn't really pay
off. Disney tried to make her a thing a
few years ago, and well, there's a
reason it never happens. She's got that
same blank eyed deer in the headlights
look as everything else I've ever seen
her in. And she just doesn't have the
acting range or the charisma to play a
tough intense survivor like the movie
needs. She stands out so badly because
everyone else is doing their absolute
level best to sell this one. In terms of
the direction and cinematography, I've
got no issues here. D Costa steers this
movie with confidence and artistic
flare. Yeah, she definitely takes her
cues from Danny Bole's playbook in terms
of shots, framing, and editing style,
which kind of makes me wonder why Bole
himself didn't just direct it, but
whatever. Credit where credit's due. The
Bone Temple looks good, the action's
well framed, and I think a film like
this is much better suited to her
sensibilities as a filmmaker. It really
goes to show just how badly she was
chewed up and spit out by the Marvel
machine. And holy [ __ ] man. If you like
gory horror and torture scenes, you're
in for a treat here. That's for sure.
It's not often that I find myself
wanting to look away from the screen
because what I'm seeing is just so
[ __ ] grim, but this movie managed it
a few times in the first act. It does
settle down a bit after that to be fair,
which kind of makes me wonder if it had
something to prove early on. Plot-wise,
The Bone Temple isn't a complicated
movie, but then this franchise never
really is. It's always been more about
the characters and themes than the
actual story, which is fine when
characters like Jimmy and Kelson are
driving the action, but it can get a
little bit bogged down and slow paced
when they're not. It doesn't do much to
expand the world building or take us
anywhere we haven't seen already. And if
you're expecting a resolution to the
story between Spike and his father,
you're [ __ ] out of luck because that guy
never even gets mentioned here. Like I
said before, I can't shake the feeling
that this is more like an add-on to the
previous film than a well-rounded
narrative of its own. Much as I loved
the first act of 28 Years Later, if you
cut that out, it probably would have
left enough time to fit both movies into
one narratively complete film instead of
two slightly disjointed halves. As it
stands though, The Bone Temple is a damn
good horror movie with some genuinely
gory moments, strong character work, and
great performances that ties up most of
the loose ends from the last film, but
also leaves the door open for the next
chapter with an ending that kind of
comes out of nowhere, but is definitely
going to get fans talking. Am I super
excited to spend more time in this
universe? Not exactly, if I'm honest,
but I'll probably watch it to see how it
ends. And if you're a fan of the
franchise, you should, too.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
